name: Zhang Guolao
description: Zhang Guolao is one of the Eight Immortals in the Taoist pantheon, revered for his eccentric nature, profound wisdom, and association with longevity. He is typically depicted as an elderly man, often riding a white donkey backwards. This unconventional mode of transportation symbolizes his detachment from worldly concerns and his ability to see things from a unique perspective. Zhang Guolao is believed to have lived during the Tang Dynasty, although his true identity remains shrouded in mystery, blurring the lines between historical figure and mythological persona. He is associated with the arts of alchemy and divination, embodying the Taoist pursuit of immortality and spiritual enlightenment. His image serves as a potent reminder of the value of unconventional thinking and the importance of seeking deeper meaning in life beyond superficial appearances. Stories portray him as possessing magical abilities, including the power to make himself invisible and transform objects.
subtitle: The Eccentric Immortal

keyMyths.title: Zhang Guolao and Emperor Xuanzong
keyMyths.description: Emperor Xuanzong of the Tang Dynasty, known for his interest in the occult, summoned Zhang Guolao to his court. The Emperor, skeptical of Zhang's claims of immortality, subjected him to various tests. Zhang Guolao, however, demonstrated his supernatural abilities, impressing the Emperor. He even refused to marry the Emperor's sister, claiming he was too old and unsuitable. This myth highlights Zhang Guolao's independence and his refusal to be bound by earthly expectations, even those of an Emperor.
keyMyths.source: Werner, E.T.C. *Myths and Legends of China*. George G. Harrap & Co., 1922.

keyMyths.title: The Donkey That Could Fold
keyMyths.description: One of Zhang Guolao's signature attributes is his white donkey, which he could magically fold up like a piece of paper and store in his pocket. When he wished to ride again, he would simply unfold the donkey, sprinkle it with water, and it would return to its normal form. This myth emphasizes Zhang Guolao's mastery over transformation and his ability to manipulate the natural world through Taoist magic. It represents the power of inner transformation and the ability to overcome limitations.
keyMyths.source: Williams, C.A.S. *Chinese Symbolism and Art Motives*. Tuttle Publishing, 2006.

keyMyths.title: The Elixir of Immortality
keyMyths.description: Legends say that Zhang Guolao possessed the secret to creating the elixir of immortality. He was said to have spent much of his time in remote mountains, searching for the necessary ingredients and refining his alchemical techniques. While the exact recipe for his elixir remains a mystery, the myth underscores the Taoist belief in the possibility of achieving physical and spiritual immortality through disciplined practice and the understanding of natural forces. It also reinforces his role as a guide on the path to enlightenment.
keyMyths.source: Eberhard, Wolfram. *A Dictionary of Chinese Symbols: Hidden Symbols in Chinese Life and Thought*. Routledge, 2013.

sources.author: Werner, E.T.C.
sources.description: A comprehensive collection of Chinese myths and legends, providing valuable insights into the stories surrounding Zhang Guolao.
